bibliographic.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'An English Friend of Charles of Orleans,' Publications of the Modern Language Association of America 26.1 (1911), 142–180.
bibliographic.container_title: Publications of the Modern Language Association of America
bibliographic.title: An English Friend of Charles of Orleans

entities.description: The immediate source article. CME00078 selects sections B–E on pp. 155–180 and omits section A on pp. 142–155. MacCracken supplies manuscript-based transcriptions, source notes, cross-comparisons, and an argument for William de la Pole's authorship.
entities.label: Henry Noble MacCracken, An English Friend of Charles of Orleans (1911)

notes: Section B begins partway down p. 155; C begins on p. 174, D on p. 178, and E on p. 179. The article and encoded selection end on p. 180.

rights.basis: Published in the United States in 1911; the printed article is in the U.S. public domain. Internet Archive access terms and scan metadata remain separate layers.

bibliographic.citation: Aimé Champollion-Figeac, ed., Les poésies du duc Charles d'Orléans publiées sur le manuscrit de la Bibliothèque de Grenoble, conféré avec ceux de Paris et de Londres (Paris: J. Belin-Leprieur et Colomb de Batines, 1842).
bibliographic.publisher: J. Belin-Leprieur et Colomb de Batines
bibliographic.title: Les poésies du duc Charles d'Orléans

entities.description: An edition based principally on the Grenoble manuscript and compared with manuscripts in Paris and London. MacCracken reprints section C from pp. 265–270 and section D from pp. 455–456. Champollion's notes identify Le Roux de Lincy's copies of English pieces and Jules Delpit's copies from Royal 16 F II.
entities.label: Aimé Champollion-Figeac, Les poésies du duc Charles d'Orléans (1842)

entities.description: A provisionally modeled documentary intermediary: Champollion's note 73 says Antoine Le Roux de Lincy copied the English pieces for him. No surviving standalone transcript was identified in this research.
entities.label: Le Roux de Lincy's working copies of English poems for Champollion
notes: The schema has no dedicated working-transcript entity type; catalog_description is used provisionally without implying that a modern catalog record survives.

entities.description: A provisionally modeled documentary intermediary: Champollion states that Jules Delpit copied the English pieces from Royal MS 16 F II and sent them to Paris.
entities.label: Jules Delpit's working copies from Royal MS 16 F II
notes: The exact form and present survival of Delpit's copy have not been established.

dateStatements.label: current catalog date
dateStatements.value: likely written in the 1440s, possibly completed 1450

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century parchment miscellany. The anonymous twenty-poem Fairfax Sequence occupies ff. 318r–329r and is MacCracken's section B source.

holding.notes: The current catalog identifies the Fairfax Sequence as anonymous and gives exact folios.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Fairfax 16

physicalDescription.extent: ff. i + 340 + i; current complete digital object also includes binding surfaces
notes: The complete digital object has 721 canvases. The target begins on f. 318r and ends on f. 329r; XML's first explicit folio milestone is 318v because the opening recto is implicit.

dateStatements.label: principal production period
dateStatements.notes: The date of the complete manuscript does not by itself date every English addition.

entities.description: Charles d'Orléans's complex personal poetry manuscript, containing English pieces among predominantly French material. All nine section C incipits were visually confirmed on manuscript pp. 310–313 and 346.

holding.notes: The Gallica object supplies a complete 606-canvas digital surrogate.

entities.label: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Français 25458

physicalDescription.extent: 537 numbered pages plus inserted blanks and end material
notes: The target is discontiguous: seven poems on pp. 310–313 and two poems on p. 346.
physicalDescription.support: paper and parchment components described in the official record

entities.description: A complete Charles d'Orléans poetry manuscript digitized by Pagella. It was Champollion's principal stated manuscript, but no target-poem overlap for CME00078 section C has been established; it is retained as source-evaluation evidence rather than a direct ancestor of the English selection.

holding.notes: Legacy catalog number 873; the complete IIIF object has 330 canvases.

entities.label: Grenoble, Bibliothèque municipale, U. 1091 Rés.

holding.notes: No complete public digital surrogate was found.

entities.description: A defective fifteenth- to sixteenth-century paper household anthology associated with Findern, Derbyshire. MacCracken's section E poem II occupies historical f. 178r, mapped by direct visual comparison to current CUDL f. 154r and IIIF canvas 311.

holding.notes: Current CUDL TEI and IIIF identifiers are recorded in the access entry.

entities.label: Cambridge, University Library, MS Ff.1.6 (Findern manuscript)

notes: The manuscript is defective, with 29 leaves reported missing. The complete current digital object has 329 canvases.

access.accessMethod: Request a complete color reproduction of C 47/34/1/4 and its relevant enclosure or physical context, citing the exact reference and Sobecki's 2025 article.
access.format: Repository-supplied digital copy, subject to condition and quotation
notes: The initial page check starts at £9.92, after which TNA supplies a copy quotation; whole or high-volume requests are handled separately.

rights.basis: No copy or delivery license has been obtained. TNA's record-copying terms govern supplied copies, restrict further reproduction without permission, and must be reviewed with any quotation or delivery.

entities.description: A newly reported mid-fifteenth-century copy of 'A Balade colourd and reversid' on a reused oblong paper strip in the Chancery Miscellanea. It is an earlier collateral witness to section E poem I, not a source MacCracken used.

holding.notes: The exact piece-level public catalog page was not identified. The public C 47 browse record is a parent-series route rather than an exact item record; request by the full reference.

entities.label: Kew, The National Archives, C 47/34/1/4

notes: Sobecki describes an informal trained mid-fifteenth-century Anglicana hand.

next_steps: Collate all nine Champollion readings against BnF français 25458, Grenoble U.1091, and the relevant Carpentras manuscript.
Locate surviving Le Roux de Lincy or Champollion papers and determine whether one or several working transcripts intervened.
Replace the provisional catalog_description intermediary if the actual transcript survives and can be described precisely.
openQuestions.question: What exact manuscript and working-copy chain underlies each section C poem, given MacCracken's 'Grenoble' label, Champollion's references to La Vallière, Carpentras, and Le Roux de Lincy's copies, and modern confirmation in BnF français 25458?

next_steps: Build a poem-level concordance to modern JONAS, DIMEV, Fox–Arn, Pearsall, and Neilly records.
Keep MacCracken's common Suffolk attribution historical until individual assignments are supported.
openQuestions.question: Which individual poems can current scholarship securely assign to William de la Pole, Charles d'Orléans, or another author, and which remain anonymous?

next_steps: Ask whether the legacy full Royal digital object will return and request its stable IIIF or download URL.

If ordering, request each complete present physical object, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, edges, and detached matter where offered; identify target folios separately.
Retain quotations, delivery inventories, correspondence, and exact licenses with any supplied files.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library restore or supply complete color digital facsimiles of Royal MS 16 F II and Arundel MS 26, and what exact storage, Git-publication, viewer, print-on-demand, and commercial permissions would govern them?

next_steps: Request the exact item by full reference and cite Sobecki 2025.
Ask for recto, verso, scale or color target, envelope or enclosure context, and any catalog metadata not visible online.
Record the delivery license before any repository publication.
openQuestions.question: Can TNA supply a complete color reproduction of C 47/34/1/4 with enough enclosure and physical context to document the reused strip, and what reuse terms would apply?

next_steps: Treat CC BY-NC images as noncommercial unless separate permission is granted.
Ask BnF and Grenoble for written permission or a commercial license if images themselves would be redistributed in a commercial workflow.
Keep underlying medieval text rights distinct from rights or contract terms governing modern photographs and metadata.
openQuestions.question: Do the BnF, Pagella, CUDL, and Bodleian terms permit the intended Git-hosted reference access and any downstream print-on-demand or commercial use of their manuscript images?

reviewNotes: The cataloged unit is an edited selection across four source sections, not one medieval codex, anthology, or securely single-authored work.
MacCracken's William de la Pole attribution is recorded as a disputed historical claim; current Bodleian cataloging describes the Fairfax Sequence as anonymous.

Champollion's preface and note 73 distinguish his Grenoble base from additional English pieces copied by Le Roux de Lincy. Section C is therefore not modeled as a direct transcription of Grenoble U.1091.
The nine BnF section C poems are discontiguous: seven occupy manuscript pp. 310–313 and two occupy p. 346.
Champollion explicitly says Jules Delpit copied the Royal poems and sent them to Paris, so the working copy is retained as an intermediary rather than silently collapsed.

XML page breaks run consecutively from printed p. 155 through p. 180. Section C begins on p. 174, D on p. 178, and E on p. 179 after earlier sections on the same page, so no duplicate PB element is expected at those section boundaries.
The complete Grenoble U.1091 bundle is retained as source-evaluation evidence, but no target-poem overlap is asserted.
Complete-manuscript caches represent whole published digital objects; work_portion locators associate the target without cropping the downloaded source.

sourceLinks.format: Repository-supplied digital copy, subject to condition and quotation
notes: The initial page check starts at £9.92, after which TNA supplies a copy quotation; whole or high-volume requests are handled separately.

rights.basis: No copy or delivery license has been obtained. TNA's record-copying terms govern supplied copies, restrict further reproduction without permission, and must be reviewed with any quotation or delivery.

lineage.summary: CME00078 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from Henry Noble MacCracken's 'An English Friend of Charles of Orleans,' PMLA 26.1 (1911), but only sections B–E on printed pp. 155–180. Section B transcribes the twenty anonymous poems of the Fairfax Sequence from Bodleian MS Fairfax 16, ff. 318r–329r. Section C reprints nine English poems from Champollion-Figeac's 1842 edition, pp. 265–270: modern item-level evidence and direct image inspection locate them in BnF français 25458, pp. 310–313 and 346, while Champollion names Le Roux de Lincy's working copies and other French manuscript sources; MacCracken's simple 'Grenoble' label is therefore not treated as a direct witness claim. Section D reprints Champollion pp. 455–456, whose note identifies Jules Delpit's copies from Royal MS 16 F II; the two poems occur at ff. 118r and 131r. Section E transcribes one poem from Arundel MS 26, f. 32v, and one from the Findern manuscript, historically f. 178r and now f. 154r. A 2025 article reports an earlier collateral witness to the Arundel poem at TNA C 47/34/1/4. Complete public scans of the PMLA issue, Champollion edition, Fairfax 16, BnF français 25458, Grenoble U.1091, and Findern Ff.1.6 are cached, with target portions mapped inside each complete object where overlap is established. British Library and TNA witnesses remain requestable or unavailable rather than locally cached.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English poets; William de la Pole, Duke of Suffolk, historically proposed but unconfirmed
catalogSummary.date: Individual composition dates unresolved; surviving witnesses range from the mid-fifteenth into the early sixteenth century; exact assembled selection published 1911
catalogSummary.region: Anglo-French courtly literary networks; exact places of composition unresolved
catalogSummary.title: An English Friend of Charles of Orléans: Sections B–E

catalogingSubject.label: An English Friend of Charles of Orléans: Sections B–E
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The 33 Middle English lyrics selected as sections B–E of Henry Noble MacCracken's 1911 article and encoded as CME00078. This is an editorially assembled selection, not one securely attributable medieval work or one manuscript anthology: section B is the Fairfax Sequence; section C gathers nine English poems from manuscripts associated with Charles d'Orléans; section D prints two lyrics from Royal MS 16 F II; and section E juxtaposes poems from Arundel MS 26 and the Findern manuscript. Modern editorial framing, French comparison texts, and the physical manuscripts are modeled in lineage rather than merged into the intellectual work.

contentParts.label: B. Poems from the Fairfax MS. 16, in the Bodleian
contentParts.summary: Twenty linked courtly poems conventionally called the Fairfax Sequence: ballades, complaints, letters, a supplication, and a concluding parlement.

contentParts.label: C. English Poems in French MSS. of Orléans
contentParts.summary: Nine short English courtly lyrics, seven on manuscript pages 310–313 and two on page 346 of BnF français 25458; the exact mixture of manuscript evidence behind Champollion's 1842 print and MacCracken's source label remains open.

contentParts.label: D. From MS. Royal 16 F II
contentParts.summary: Two courtly lyrics beginning 'My hertly love is in your governauns' and 'Ne were my trewe innocent hert.'

contentParts.label: E. Poems in Suffolk's Manner
notes: The newly reported TNA C 47/34/1/4 copy of the first poem is an earlier collateral witness, not MacCracken's source.
contentParts.summary: MacCracken's stylistic pairing of 'Honour and beaute, vertue and gentilnesse' from Arundel MS 26 with 'A mercy, Fortune, have pitee on me' from the Findern manuscript.

dateStatements.display: 1911 as the assembled selection represented by CME00078

extent.description: Four editorial sections containing 33 verse lyrics. Counts describe XML representation: 819 L elements and 128 LG elements; editorial notes are excluded from the cataloged work extent.

formStatements.description: The cataloged selection contains 33 verse lyrics represented by 819 XML line elements in 128 verse groups.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: French comparison material and modern English or French editorial prose belong to source context, not the language facet of this cataloged Middle English selection.
The XML's 2016 revision says that a French fragment was removed from the beginning. French is therefore not added as a language facet for the cataloged work.
MacCracken's labels are preserved as source structure but are not treated as proof of common authorship or a single medieval anthology.
The 819-line count is an XML representation metric; ellipses and editorially supplied or omitted text may prevent it from equaling manuscript verse-line counts.

next_steps: Compare modern editions and item-level JONAS or DIMEV records for every incipit.
Keep MacCracken's Suffolk attribution historical until poem-level evidence supports a named responsibility.
openQuestions.question: Which, if any, of the 33 lyrics can be securely assigned to William de la Pole, Charles d'Orléans, or another named poet, and should authorship be asserted poem by poem rather than for MacCracken's assembled selection?

next_steps: Collate all nine readings against BnF français 25458 and the Grenoble and Carpentras manuscripts.
Consult Le Roux de Lincy and Champollion archival papers if surviving.
openQuestions.question: What exact manuscript or working-copy chain underlies each section C poem, given MacCracken's 'Grenoble' label, Champollion's references to La Vallière, Carpentras, and Le Roux de Lincy's copies, and modern item-level evidence for BnF français 25458?

next_steps: Review current poem-level editions and dialect studies.
Do not convert witness date or place into composition metadata without argument.
openQuestions.question: What composition date and regional attribution can responsibly be assigned to each lyric rather than to the witnesses that preserve it?

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English poet or poets

assertion.notes: Current cataloging and modern scholarship do not establish one named author for the full selection. This collective anonymous responsibility is a discovery default, not proof that every poem is anonymous: some section C poems have modern Charles d'Orléans assignments that require poem-level review.

responsibilities.agentName: William de la Pole, Duke of Suffolk

assertion.notes: Retained as attribution history, not promoted to the catalog summary as confirmed authorship.

scope.description: MacCracken's proposed common authorship, strongest for the Fairfax Sequence and the two poems he labels in Suffolk's manner
Selection, transcription, ordering, headings, and notes in the 1911 source article

summaries.value: Thirty-three anonymous Middle English courtly lyrics explore service to a lady, separation, hope and despair, Fortune, mercy, fidelity, complaint, and poetic petition. MacCracken grouped twenty poems from the Fairfax Sequence with nine English poems transmitted in Charles d'Orléans's manuscript circle, two Royal manuscript lyrics, and two poems he judged stylistically similar. His proposed attribution to William de la Pole remains a historical hypothesis rather than a settled authorship.

notes: MacCracken's article begins with a section A that is not in CME00078; the supplied qualifier prevents the encoded selection from being mistaken for the complete article.
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, adapted from MacCracken's article title to identify the encoded selection boundary

MacCracken grouped twenty poems from the Fairfax Sequence with nine English poems transmitted in Charles d'Orléans's manuscript circle, two Royal manuscript lyrics, and two poems he judged stylistically similar.
